title: Proverbs 18
subtitle: The Fortified Tower; Deep Waters; Death and Life in the Tongue

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — Name of the LORD: Fortified Tower; Deep Words; Listen Before Answering

heb.paragraph: V.10: the name of the LORD is a miḡdal-ʿōz — a strong tower. Into it the righteous run urgently and are set on high (niśgāḇ — inaccessibly high). The contrast with v.11's wealthy-man's city is pointed: wealth imagines itself a wall; the LORD's name is an actual tower.

V.4: words of the mouth are deep waters — unfathomable depth, implications the speaker cannot fully anticipate. The fountain of wisdom, by contrast, is a rushing stream — overflowing, accessible, life-giving.

refs.note: You have been my refuge, a strong tower against the foe — the same fortified-tower image.
Quick to listen, slow to speak — the NT application of v.13's listening-before-answering principle.

notes.note: The name of the LORD is a fortified tower; the righteous run to it and are safe. The image of running is urgent — not a leisurely retreat but an emergency dash. The wise person's instinctive response to danger is to flee to the LORD.
The wealth of the rich — they imagine it a wall. Wealth really does provide some protection. But the protection is finite and imagined; the protection of the LORD's name is real and inexhaustible.
To answer before listening — that is folly and shame. The person who forms their answer while others are still speaking has stopped listening. Wisdom waits for understanding before responding.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The LORD's name as tower vs. the rich man's wealth as fortified city — Alter notes the comparison is exact and pointed. Both are 'fortified' places of imagined security; one is real and one is a self-deception. The wise person knows the difference.

Alter notes that answering before listening is one of Proverbs' most practically observant instructions — it addresses the specific failure of hearing an account and forming a settled opinion before the other side has been presented. The legal and social contexts both require the discipline of hearing.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The name-of-the-LORD as fortified tower is Proverbs' most concentrated statement of divine protection. Waltke notes the 'name' (shem) of the LORD encompasses his character, reputation, and covenant commitments. To run to his name is to take refuge in what God actually is.

To answer before listening is both folly and shame. Waltke notes the two consequences are social (shame before others) and cognitive (folly — actual failure of understanding). Premature judgment is not merely rude but epistemically defective.

Calvin: 'The name of the LORD is a strong tower — our security is not in our own strength or wealth but in his character, his promise, and his faithfulness. To run to his name is to trust his character.'
Calvin: 'He who answers before hearing speaks from pride. The humble person listens because they know they might be wrong; the proud person speaks first because they have already decided.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on migdal oz (strong tower) traces the fortress imagery through Ps 46:1; 61:3; 91:2; 144:2. The 'name' encompasses character and covenant commitment — it is not merely a title but the reality of who God is.

The NET note connects 18:12 ('before honour is humility') to 16:18 (pride before destruction) and 15:33 (humility before honour), identifying this as a recurring structural principle in the moral order.

hist.context: The first half of ch.18 addresses isolation (v.1), the fool's love of their own opinions (v.2), and speech dynamics (vv.4,6-8). V.10's fortified-tower saying is the theological centre: the LORD's name provides what wealth only imagines providing (v.11). V.13 is the chapter's most immediately practical saying: answering before listening is folly and shame.

sections.header: Verses 14–24 — Death and Life in the Tongue; Cross-Examine First; Friend Closer than a Brother

heb.gloss: death and life in the power of the tongue
heb.paragraph: V.21: the most explicit statement of the tongue's power in Proverbs. The tongue holds (bǝyaḏ — in the hand of) death and life. Those who love the tongue — who cultivate their speech — will eat its fruit. Speech is the most powerful tool under human control.

V.22: finds (māṣāʾ) has the same root as 8:35 — "whoever finds me finds life." The parallel is deliberate: finding a wife is structurally equivalent to finding wisdom. The wife who embodies wisdom (31:10-31) is the embodiment of the wisdom the whole book has been seeking.

refs.note: Whoever finds me finds life — the parallel structure to v.22 (finding a wife = finding what is good, receiving favour from the LORD).
I call you friends — the friend closer than a brother (v.24) finds NT fulfilment in Jesus's friendship with his disciples.

notes.note: In a lawsuit the first to speak seems right, until someone comes forward and cross-examines. Every one-sided presentation sounds compelling until the other side is heard. The wisdom argument for due process and patient hearing.

The tongue has the power of life and death. The most explicit statement of what the entire tongue cluster in Proverbs has been building toward. The wise person cultivates speech carefully: they are handling something with the force of a weapon or a medicine.

There is a friend who sticks closer than a brother. True friendship has a loyalty that surpasses the biological bond. For the Christian tradition, this is the text that points most directly to Christ — the friend whose loyalty was expressed by dying in the place of the friend.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Death and life in the tongue — Alter notes the word order (death before life) is unusual and may signal that the negative consequence is the more immediate danger. Those who love to talk will eat whatever fruit their talk produces — a warning as much as a promise.

The first account always seems right — Alter notes this is Proverbs' most explicitly legal proverb. Applied beyond legal contexts: never form a final opinion on one party's account. The instruction is universally applicable.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The tongue has power of life and death — Waltke notes this is Proverbs' most extreme speech-ethics statement. The claim is literal: words produce outcomes that extend to life and death, both the speaker's and the listener's. The 'fruit of the tongue' is the consequences of one's speech.

The friend who sticks closer than a brother — Waltke notes davaq (cling) is the covenant-loyalty word of Gen 2:24 and Ruth 1:14. The exceptional friend exercises the same kind of steadfast covenant-quality loyalty that marriage and Ruth's commitment to Naomi embody.
Calvin: 'The tongue has power of life and death — Solomon is not hyperbolic. Words make friendships and break them, heal and destroy. James 3 is the NT commentary on this verse.'

Calvin on the friend who sticks closer than a brother: 'This points toward Christ, who is a better friend than any natural bond of blood. The natural brother may abandon you; the friend of this quality never does.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on mavet vechayyim beyad lashon (death and life in the hand of the tongue) discusses the instrumental beyad — the tongue wields its power as instruments in the speaker's hands. The note cites Jas 3:5–12 as the NT development.
The NET note on davaq (sticks closer) identifies the covenant-loyalty word from Gen 2:24 and Ruth 1:14. The exceptional friend exercises hesed-quality loyalty.

hist.context: The second half contains the chapter's most famous verse: death and life in the power of the tongue (v.21). Preceded by the legal principle that cross-examination is essential (v.17) and followed by the deep friendship saying (v.24). The chapter closes with the friend who sticks closer than a brother — pointing beyond itself to Christ.

ppl.name: The righteous
ppl.text: The instinctive response to danger: flee to the real tower, not the imagined wealth-wall.

ppl.name: The tongue-lover
ppl.role: Eats its fruit — life or death
ppl.text: Speech is cultivated; its harvest depends on how it is tended.

ppl.name: The friend closer than a brother

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"The tongue has the power of life and death, and those who love it will eat its fruit."NoteThe most explicit statement of speech's power in Proverbs. The tongue is not merely a social tool but a life-and-death instrument.

src.title: Fortified tower
src.quote: Miḡdal-ʿōz — the last refuge when a city falls. Running to it was the emergency action of a population under attack.
src.note: The military metaphor grounds the theology in experiential urgency.

src.title: Friend closer than a brother
src.quote: Rēaʿ (friend) — a closer, more chosen relationship than the biological ʾāḥ (brother). The most intimate bond in wisdom's social vision.
src.note: The saying has generated extensive theological reflection on divine friendship.

rec.text: Death and life in the power of the tongue — the most cited Proverbs verse in Christian preaching on speech ethics.
Friend closer than a brother — cited by Augustine, Bernard of Clairvaux, and Aelred of Rievaulx, and applied Christologically throughout Christian devotional literature.

rows.label: Fortified Tower vs Wealthy Person's Walls
rows.text: The name of the LORD is a fortified tower; the righteous run to it and are safe. The wealthy person's wealth is their fortified city -- they imagine it a wall too high to scale. Two towers: one real, one imagined.

rows.label: Fortified Tower vs Wealthy Person's Walls
rows.text: The name of the LORD is a fortified tower; the righteous run to it and are safe. The wealthy person's wealth is their fortified city -- they imagine it a wall too high to scale. Two towers: one real, one imagined.

rows.label: Death and Life in the Tongue — 18:21
rows.text: The most extreme statement of Proverbs' speech ethics: the tongue literally wields life and death as instruments. The claim is structural — words produce outcomes that extend to life and death. The wise person handles their tongue as they would handle a weapon.

hebtext.note: V.10: the name of the LORD is a miḡdal-ʿōz — a strong tower. Into it the righteous run urgently and are set on high (niśgāḇ — inaccessibly high). The contrast with v.11's wealthy-man's city is pointed: wealth imagines itself a wall; the LORD's name is an actual tower.

V.4: words of the mouth are deep waters — unfathomable depth, implications the speaker cannot fully anticipate. The fountain of wisdom, by contrast, is a rushing stream — overflowing, accessible, life-giving.

hebtext.gloss: death and life in the power of the tongue
hebtext.note: V.21: the most explicit statement of the tongue's power in Proverbs. The tongue holds (bǝyaḏ — in the hand of) death and life. Those who love the tongue — who cultivate their speech — will eat its fruit. Speech is the most powerful tool under human control.

V.22: finds (māṣāʾ) has the same root as 8:35 — "whoever finds me finds life." The parallel is deliberate: finding a wife is structurally equivalent to finding wisdom. The wife who embodies wisdom (31:10-31) is the embodiment of the wisdom the whole book has been seeking.

tx.title: 'Death and life in the hand of the tongue'
tx.content: MT: mavet vechayyim beyad lashon (death and life in the hand of the tongue). The word order — death before life — is unusual. LXX: thanatos kai zōē en cheiri glōssēs — identical meaning.
tx.note: The verse's unqualified claim is Proverbs' most extreme speech statement. The 'fruit of the tongue' the tongue-lover eats encompasses both life-giving and death-dealing consequences. James 3:5–12 is the NT commentary.

debate.title: 'The Friend Who Sticks Closer Than a Brother' — Christological Reference?

positions.name: A rare but genuinely human friend, primarily
positions.argument: The proverb describes an exceptional human friend whose loyalty exceeds even the primary blood-bond. The application to Christ is a legitimate homiletical extension of the primary meaning.

positions.name: Points toward Christ; the ideal exceeds the human
positions.argument: The friend who exceeds even a brother in closeness points beyond any human relationship to Christ, who never abandons. The ideal described exceeds what any human friend can fully achieve.

scores.label: The LORD's Name as True Security
Death and Life in the Tongue

themes.note: Proverbs 18 develops social dynamics with particular attention to speech and security. The most extreme observation — death and life in the tongue — is balanced by the most grounding — the LORD's name as fortified tower. Between these poles: listen before judging, hear both sides, and recognise that the tongue's power requires wisdom-formed governance.

prayer_prompt: The name of the LORD is a fortified tower; the righteous run to it and are safe. Consider asking God about your refuge — where you run when threatened, His name or something else.

coaching.tip: 'The name of the LORD is a fortified tower; the righteous run to it and are safe' (v. 10). The name represents the person — God's character, promises, presence. In danger, the wise don't rely on wealth (v. 11) but on divine protection. The tower imagery suggests both refuge and elevation — safety and perspective.

'The tongue has the power of life and death, and those who love it will eat its fruit' (v. 21). Words create realities. They can kill relationships, reputations, hope — or they can give life, encouragement, direction. What you speak, you eventually harvest. The tongue is a garden that yields what you plant.
